Project Python Foundations: FoodHub Data Analysis¶

Context¶

The number of restaurants in New York is increasing day by day. Lots of students and busy professionals rely on those restaurants due to their hectic lifestyles. Online food delivery service is a great option for them. It provides them with good food from their favorite restaurants. A food aggregator company FoodHub offers access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone app.

The app allows the restaurants to receive a direct online order from a customer. The app assigns a delivery person from the company to pick up the order after it is confirmed by the restaurant. The delivery person then uses the map to reach the restaurant and waits for the food package. Once the food package is handed over to the delivery person, he/she confirms the pick-up in the app and travels to the customer's location to deliver the food. The delivery person confirms the drop-off in the app after delivering the food package to the customer. The customer can rate the order in the app. The food aggregator earns money by collecting a fixed margin of the delivery order from the restaurants.

Objective¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in their online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of different restaurants which will help them in enhancing their customer experience. Suppose you are hired as a Data Scientist in this company and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company to improve the business.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different data related to a food order. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary¶

  • order_id: Unique ID of the order
  • customer_id: ID of the customer who ordered the food
  • restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant
  • cuisine_type: Cuisine ordered by the customer
  • cost_of_the_order: Cost of the order
  • day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order is placed on a weekday or weekend (The weekday is from Monday to Friday and the weekend is Saturday and Sunday)
  • rating: Rating given by the customer out of 5
  • food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the restaurant's order confirmation and the delivery person's pick-up confirmation.
  • delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery person to deliver the food package. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the delivery person's pick-up confirmation and drop-off information

Let us start by importing the required libraries¶

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

Univariate Analysis¶

The dataset provides an insight of the distribution of each variable that consist of visualized numerical variables with histograms, boxplots, and categorical varibles with countplots. however, at the end, key obervation will be taking from univariate analysis.¶

  • First, exploring numerical variables
  • Visualize the distributions of numerical variables.Using histograms and boxplots to visualize distribution of numerical variables to understand their central tendency, identify outliers, and spread.
In [14]:
# Aesthetic style of the plots set
sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Creating subplots for cost_of_the_order
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))
sns.histplot(data=df, x='cost_of_the_order', kde=True, ax=axes[0]);
axes[0].set_title('Distribution of Cost of the Order')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Cost of the Order')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Frequency')
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='cost_of_the_order', ax=axes[1]);
axes[1].set_title('Box Plot of Cost of the Order')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Cost of the Order')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [16]:
# Creating subplots for food_preparation_time
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))
sns.histplot(data=df, x='food_preparation_time', kde=True, ax=axes[0]);
axes[0].set_title('Distribution of Food Preparation Time')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Food Preparation Time (minutes)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Frequency')
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='food_preparation_time', ax=axes[1]);
axes[1].set_title('Box Plot of Food Preparation Time')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Food Preparation Time (minutes)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Exploring categorical variables.

  • Visualizing the distributions of categorical variables using countplots to get understanding of the frequency of each category in the data.
In [19]:
# Creating figures and subplots
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(15, 10))

# Ploting for restaurant_name (Top 10)
sns.countplot(data=df, y='restaurant_name', order=df['restaurant_name'].value_counts().index[:10], ax=axes[0, 0], palette='viridis', hue='restaurant_name', legend=False);
axes[0, 0].set_title('Top 10 Restaurants by Order Count')
axes[0, 0].set_xlabel('Number of Orders')
axes[0, 0].set_ylabel('Restaurant Name')


# Plotting for cuisine_type
sns.countplot(data=df, y='cuisine_type', order=df['cuisine_type'].value_counts().index, ax=axes[0, 1], palette='viridis', hue='cuisine_type', legend=False);
axes[0, 1].set_title('Distribution of Cuisine Type')
axes[0, 1].set_xlabel('Number of Orders')
axes[0, 1].set_ylabel('Cuisine Type')


# Plotting for day_of_the_week
sns.countplot(data=df, x='day_of_the_week', order=df['day_of_the_week'].value_counts().index, ax=axes[1, 0], palette='viridis', hue='day_of_the_week', legend=False);
axes[1, 0].set_title('Distribution of Day of the Week')
axes[1, 0].set_xlabel('Day of the Week')
axes[1, 0].set_ylabel('Number of Orders')

# Plotting for rating
sns.countplot(data=df, x='rating', order=df['rating'].value_counts().index, ax=axes[1, 1], palette='viridis', hue='rating', legend=False);
axes[1, 1].set_title('Distribution of Rating')
axes[1, 1].set_xlabel('Rating')
axes[1, 1].set_ylabel('Number of Orders')


plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Analysis of Key Observations

  • The'cost_of_the_order' was right-skewed, the distributions indicate that most of the orders have lower cost. However, it is significate to note that some orders have higher costs, and the appearance can be seen as outliers in the boxplot.
  • Both 'delivery_time' and'food_preparation_time' show distributions that are symmetrical, which was around a certain time range. The boxplots show some outliers in both variables, presenting short or long delivery times and preparation.
  • The dataset indicate that the top 10 restaurants account for a significant part of the orders.
  • Some cuisine types are more popular than others, as proof of the varying bar heights in the cuisine type countplot.
  • Orders by 'day_of_the_week' distributions indicating difference between weekdays and weekends in the order volume Distributions of the 'rating' shows the frequency of different rating scores given by customers, however, it is notice that number of orders in the rating were not provided.

Univariate Analysis:

  • Understanding from the initial analysis help us with the characteristics of the data by identifying areas for further investigation, with a view to analyzing the outliers in cost and time, and exploring the some reasons for unrated orders.

Exploring relationships between numerical variables by using a correlation matrix and scatter plots.

Analying Key Observations

  • The findings show very weak direction in relationship between the numerical variables which are 'cost_of_the_order', 'food_preparation_time', and 'delivery_time', and was indicated by correlation coefficients close to zero.

  • Boxplots visualization generated the relationships that were seen between numerical variables which are ('cost_of_the_order', 'food_preparation_time', and 'delivery_time') and categorical variables ('day_of_the_week' and 'rating').

  • Disribution of these plots show how the numerical variables varies across different categories.

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer. [3 marks]¶

Filtering out 'not given' ratings:

Excluding orders with 'Not given' ratings from the analysis.

In [44]:
# Converting the 'rating' column to numerical data type
rated_df['rating'] = rated_df['rating'].astype(float)

# Group by restaurant in the data and calculate the count and average of ratings
restaurant_ratings = rated_df.groupby('restaurant_name')['rating'].agg(['count', 'mean']).reset_index()

# The columns rename for clarity
restaurant_ratings.rename(columns={'count': 'rating_count', 'mean': 'average_rating'}, inplace=True)

restaurant_ratings.head()
Out[44]:
restaurant_name rating_count average_rating
0 'wichcraft 1 5.0
1 12 Chairs 2 4.5
2 5 Napkin Burger 2 4.0
3 67 Burger 1 5.0
4 Amma 2 4.5
In [45]:
# Filtering for restaurants with more than 50 ratings in the data
high_rating_count_restaurants = restaurant_ratings[restaurant_ratings['rating_count'] > 50]

# More filtering for restaurants with an average rating greater than 4 in the data
qualifying_restaurants = high_rating_count_restaurants[high_rating_count_restaurants['average_rating'] > 4]

qualifying_restaurants
Out[45]:
restaurant_name rating_count average_rating

Observations:¶

  • Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken, Blue Ribbon Sushi, Shake Shack, and The Meatball Shop are the restaurants that met the rating count greater than 50 and average rating greater than 4.

  • These four specific restaurants could be a target for special promotional offers because of their high volume of ratings and positive feedback.

Question 14: The company charges the restaurant 25% on the orders having cost greater than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars. Find the net revenue generated by the company across all orders. [3 marks]¶

In [47]:
def calculate_commission(cost):
    """
    Calculates the commission for an order based on its cost.

    Args:
        cost (float): The cost of the order.

    Returns:
        float: The calculated commission.
    """
    if cost > 20:
        return cost * 0.25
    elif cost > 5:
        return cost * 0.15
    else:
        return 0
In [48]:
# Applying the calculate_commission function to create the 'commission' column
df['commission'] = df['cost_of_the_order'].apply(calculate_commission)

df.head()
Out[48]:
order_id customer_id restaurant_name cuisine_type cost_of_the_order day_of_the_week rating food_preparation_time delivery_time commission

1 1477685 358141 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya Japanese 12.08 Weekend Not given 25 23 1.8120
2 1477070 66393 Cafe Habana Mexican 12.23 Weekday 5 23 28 1.8345
3 1477334 106968 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken American 29.20 Weekend 3 25 15 7.3000
4 1478249 76942 Dirty Bird to Go American 11.59 Weekday 4 25 24 1.7385

# Calculating the total net revenue
net_revenue = df['commission'].sum()

f"Total Net Revenue: ${net_revenue:.2f}"
Out[50]:
'Total Net Revenue: $6166.30'
In [51]:
f"Total Net Revenue: ${net_revenue:.2f}"
Out[51]:
'Total Net Revenue: $6166.30'

Observations:¶

  • The commission was calculated based on the cost: 25% for orders costing more than $20 and 15% for orders costing more than $5.

  • According to the result of the output, the total net revenue generated by the company across all orders is $6166.30.

Question 15: The company wants to analyze the total time required to deliver the food. What percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes to get delivered from the time the order is placed? (The food has to be prepared and then delivered.) [2 marks]¶

In [52]:
df['total_delivery_time'] = df['food_preparation_time'] + df['delivery_time']

df.head()
Out[52]:
order_id customer_id restaurant_name cuisine_type cost_of_the_order day_of_the_week rating food_preparation_time delivery_time commission total_delivery_time
0 1477147 337525 Hangawi Korean 30.75 Weekend Not given 25 20 7.6875 45
1 1477685 358141 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya Japanese 12.08 Weekend Not given 25 23 1.8120 48
2 1477070 66393 Cafe Habana Mexican 12.23 Weekday 5 23 28 1.8345 51
3 1477334 106968 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken American 29.20 Weekend 3 25 15 7.3000 40
4 1478249 76942 Dirty Bird to Go American 11.59 Weekday 4 25 24 1.7385 49
In [53]:
# Calculating the number of orders that took more than 60 minutes
orders_over_60_minutes = df[df['total_delivery_time'] > 60].shape[0]

# Calculating the percentage of orders that took more than 60 minutes
percentage_over_60_minutes = (orders_over_60_minutes / df.shape[0]) * 100

f"Percentage of orders taking more than 60 minutes to deliver: {percentage_over_60_minutes:.2f}%"
Out[53]:
'Percentage of orders taking more than 60 minutes to deliver: 10.54%'

Observations:¶

  • total_delivery_time, was added as a new column, representing the sum of food_preparation_time and delivery_time.
  • 10.54% of orders had a total delivery time exceeding 60 minutes.

Question 16: The company wants to analyze the delivery time of the orders on weekdays and weekends. How does the mean delivery time vary during weekdays and weekends? [2 marks]¶

In [54]:
mean_delivery_time_by_day = df.groupby('day_of_the_week')['delivery_time'].mean()

mean_delivery_time_by_day
Out[54]:
delivery_time
day_of_the_week
Weekday 28.340037
Weekend 22.470022

Observations:¶

The mean delivery time on weekdays is approximately 28.34 minutes, while on weekends it is approximately 22.47 minutes. However, it shows that the average delivery time is longer during weekdays compared to weekends

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Question 17: What are your conclusions from the analysis? What recommendations would you like to share to help improve the business? (You can use cuisine type and feedback ratings to drive your business recommendations.) [6 marks]¶

Conclusions:¶

  • Based on the number of orders, it was evident that American cusine was popular on weekends. The Meatball Shop and Shake Shack restaurants had high volume of orders in the data.

  • The order costs distribution was skewed based on lower values, presenting a smaller number of high-cost orders in the data.

  • The distribution that was analyzed for food preparation and delivery times, show the presentation of some outliers. Calculation was done on the percentage of orders that exceed 60 minutes for the total delivery time.

  • It is evidently shown that customer ratings had some number of orders that were not rated. Within these rated orders, some restaurants were identified with a high average rating and high rating count, which can arise as a potential for promotional offers.

  • Apparently, based on the commission structure, revenue generation was calculated on net revenue by the company from the data.

Recommendations:¶

  • Marketing campaigns can be tool for designing or highlighting the most popular cuisines. These restaurants can take advantage on the demand.
  • Leverage most be Targeted towards popular cuisine types and top-performing restaurants to attract more customers and increase order volume. For example, American on weekends with special promotional offers. Restaurants with high average ratings and high rating counts like The Meatball Shop, Shake Shack, Blue Ribbon Sushi, and Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken qualify for such offers.
  • Implementing strategies to Improve rating Participation by encourage customers to rate their orders. They can provide incentives such as small discounts for future order and a loyalty program for rewarding active reviewers.
  • Analyzing and investigating the factors that might be contributing to longer delivery times and food preparation. For example, this could be delay of preparation time or logistics delivery.
  • You can Set Service Level Agreements (SLAs) to optimize food preparation times and delivery to improve efficiency and customer satisfaction.
  • Investigate the high-cost orders to understand customer behavior and look for opportunities to introduce some promotions or premium services.
  • Share insights by utilizing feedbacks for Improvemen from customer ratings. and also feedbacks from restaurants to help them improve their service quality, food preparation, and service delivery. Restaurants with high ratings, can identify their best practices models and share them with others.

With optimization, and driving business growth, FoodHub can Improve customer experience, by implementing these recommendations.
